paper.title: STARE: Surprisal-Guided Token-Level Advantage Reweighting for Policy Entropy Stability

claims.claim: Per-token entropy variation under GRPO decomposes into the product of trajectory-level advantage Â and a local entropy sensitivity function Φ determined by the next-token distribution.
evidence: Theorem 3.1: dH/dt ∝ Â · Φ(p) in the unclipped GRPO regime
Derivation uses softmax Jacobian, chain rule from logits to entropy via Lemma 2.1
Full proof in Appendix D.4

claims.claim: There exists a unique critical surprisal threshold 𝔰* that separates entropy-increasing and entropy-decreasing tokens under GRPO.
evidence: Proposition 3.2: Φ(p) > 0 when 𝔰 > 𝔰*, Φ(p) < 0 when 𝔰 < 𝔰*
Proof of uniqueness via monotonicity of Φ(p) in surprisal
Full proof in Appendix E.3

claims.claim: The advantage-surprisal space partitions into a four-quadrant structure determining per-token entropy contribution direction.
evidence: Corollary 3.3: 2×2 matrix of advantage × surprisal → 4 quadrants (U⁺, L⁺, U⁻, L⁻)
Each quadrant has unambiguous entropy effect: L⁺ (+), U⁺ (-), L⁻ (-), U⁻ (+)
Proof in Appendix E.4

claims.claim: In positive-advantage trajectories, low-surprisal (high-probability) tokens dominate sampling frequency and drive most entropy-decreasing updates, while rare high-surprisal tokens that could raise entropy are diluted.
evidence: Appendix E.5: Asymmetric Entropy Contributions under Shared Trajectory-Level Advantages
The sampling probability p = π(a|c) implies low-surprisal tokens are sampled far more often
Empirical validation: entropy collapse observed in GRPO-ds training runs

claims.claim: The entropy neutrality identity holds: E_a~π[Φ(a)] = 0.
evidence: Theorem 3.4: Σ_v π_v Φ(π_v) = 0
Proof via algebraic manipulation of entropy sensitivity function definition
Full proof in Appendix E.6

claims.claim: A near-criticality property: a mild token-level weight perturbation (W slightly > 1) suffices to flip the sign of entropy evolution, and the critical threshold W* ≈ 1 + O(T⁻¹).
evidence: Proposition 3.5: dH̄/dη|_W = -(1/N)[Λ - (W-1)Γ]
Corollary 3.6: W* - 1 = Λ/Γ = O(T⁻¹) for large T and batch size
Empirical validation: W=1.01 already mitigates entropy decay; W≥1.05 yields steady growth (Figure 4a)
Full proof in Appendices E.7-E.11

claims.claim: Without intervention, entropy collapse is self-reinforcing: entropy reduction → distribution concentrates → high-surprisal tokens diluted → further entropy reduction.
evidence: Section 3.4: Cross-step dynamics analysis
Formalized in Appendix F (mean-field approximation, open-loop collapse)
Empirical: GRPO-ds entropy collapses to near-zero by step 1000 (Figures 3, 5-8)

claims.claim: Batch-internal top-P% surprisal quantile reliably approximates the theoretical critical surprisal threshold 𝔰* for identifying entropy-critical tokens.
evidence: Validation experiment (Figure 11a): ~60% → ~95% agreement with 𝔰* during training
Figure 11b: cumulative net entropy contribution of L_q⁺ remains positive and monotonic
Appendix B.2: detailed validation

claims.claim: STARE sustains policy entropy within a target band during RL training over thousands of steps.
evidence: Figure 3a: entropy held near H_tgt=0.3 for 5000 steps (7B Short CoT)
Figures 5-8: consistent across 1.5B-32B, Short/Long CoT, Tool-Use
Figure 4b: closed-loop gate holds entropy in target band for W ∈ [1.05, 1.5]

claims.claim: STARE outperforms DAPO and other competitive baselines by 4-8% on AIME24 and AIME25.
evidence: Short CoT 7B: STARE-O1 44.2% (AIME24), 23.8% (AIME25) vs DAPO ~34%/~17%
Improvements of ~10% and ~7% respectively over DAPO
Table 2: all 8 STARE variants outperform GRPO-ds
Long CoT 1.5B: +10.8% over DAPO
Tool-Use 7B: +5.5% over GRPO-ds

claims.claim: STARE-C2 (dual-sided: amplify L⁺ + attenuate L⁻) provides further gains over STARE-O1 in some settings.
evidence: Long CoT 8B: STARE-C2 62.2% vs STARE-O1 62.0%
Tool-Use 7B: STARE-C2 60.4% vs STARE-O1 59.4%
Table 2: C2 achieves 42.5%/24.2% vs O1 44.2%/23.8% on AIME24/25

claims.claim: The tokens selected by STARE for advantage amplification concentrate on reflection and self-correction vocabulary (uncertainty tokens, forking semantics).
evidence: Figure 10: word cloud showing 'should be', 'but', 'instead', 'verification'
Figure 13: STARE surpasses GRPO-ds on reflection/self-correction categories
Response length grows in tandem with reflection tokens (Figure 3d)

claims.claim: Target-entropy closed-loop gating substantially reduces sensitivity to the reweighting factor W and selection ratio P.
evidence: Figure 4b: all W ∈ [1.05, 1.5] steer entropy into target band with bounded oscillation
Figure 4c: P ∈ [5%, 40%] all prevent entropy collapse
Without gate (Figure 4a): W≥2.0 triggers divergence; with gate, stable across range

claims.claim: STARE achieves this with a minimal modification to the GRPO objective — only reweighting effective advantages of a selected token subset.
evidence: Section 4.2: only the effective advantage Â_i is scaled/attenuated for the selected subset
The clipped surrogate GRPO objective is otherwise unchanged
Algorithm C in Appendix: pseudocode shows minimal change from GRPO
